articles.title: Study explores how cultural tightness shapes personality and political beliefs
articles.summary: Living in a region with strict social norms is linked to distinct psychological traits. A new study found that culturally tight societies often show higher levels of right-wing authoritarianism, racial stereotyping, and a greater need for order.
articles.aiSummary: Living in a region with strict social norms is linked to distinct psychological traits. A new study found that culturally tight societies often show higher levels of right-wing authoritarianism, racial stereotyping, and a greater need for order.

articles.title: Accelerometry-Derived Digital Biomarkers for Cardiometabolic Risk: A Population-Representative Tabular Benchmark with Uncertainty Quantification
articles.summary: arXiv:2606.30702v1 Announce Type: new Abstract: Structured tabular data dominates clinical medicine, yet existing benchmarks fail to reflect real-world properties like complex survey sampling, demographic oversampling, and subgroup fairness. We introduce the NHANES Accelerometry Cardiometabolic Benchmark, derived from NHANES 2003-2006, comprising 1,381 adults with hip-worn accelerometry, fasting laboratory biomarkers, dietary intake, and anthropometrics. We evaluate three tabular learning methods -- ridge regression, XGBoost, and the foundation model TabPFN v2 -- to predict glycated haemoglobi

articles.title: From Search to Synthesis: Training LLMs as Zero-Shot Workflow Generators
articles.summary: arXiv:2606.30704v1 Announce Type: new Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) excel across a wide range of tasks, yet their instance-specific solutions often lack the structural consistency needed for reliable deployment. Workflows that encode recurring algorithmic patterns at the task level provide a principled framework, offering robustness across instance variations, interpretable traces for debugging, and reusability across problem instances. However, manually designing such workflows requires significant expertise and effort, limiting their broader application. While automatic workflow gen

articles.title: Why Do Few-Step Text Latents Fail When Image Latents Work? Non-Commitment at Sharp Categorical Readouts
articles.summary: arXiv:2606.30705v1 Announce Type: new Abstract: Deterministic few-step generation succeeds on continuous image latents but collapses to incoherent text on continuous text latents, and we show the cause is geometric rather than a training or scaling deficiency: a smooth, regularity-limited deterministic map cannot resolve a discrete branch choice before a sharp categorical readout, so few-step failure is governed by decoder sharpness, not transport accuracy. In the overlapping regime of real text autoencoders, we prove (Theorem 3) that the posterior-mean terminal step flips tokens at the rate o

articles.title: Stationary covariance spectra of discrete-time non-normal random recurrent dynamics
articles.summary: arXiv:2606.31944v1 Announce Type: new Abstract: Principal component analysis is widely used to characterize structure in the dynamics of recurrent neural networks. For stationary noise-driven dynamics, the distribution of variance among the principal components is determined by the spectrum of the stationary covariance matrix. While the spectral properties of this matrix are well-understood for linear networks with normal synaptic weight matrices, our understanding of the stationary covariance spectrum for random non-normal dynamics remains incomplete. articles.title: Scientists say creatine may help fight depression
articles.summary: Creatine is best known as a muscle-building supplement, but scientists are now investigating whether it could also help treat depression by boosting the brain's energy supply. A new review examined five randomized clinical trials involving 238 participants and found mixed results. Two studies, both involving women with major depressive disorder, reported that adding creatine to standard treatment improved symptoms, while three others found no meaningful benefit.

articles.title: How belief in karma shapes whom we choose to help
articles.summary: Belief in karma can lead people to view social inequality as fair, but it also motivates charitable giving. New research reveals that focusing on karma increases generosity, but mostly toward individuals who suffer through no fault of their own.
articles.aiSummary: Belief in karma can lead people to view social inequality as fair, but it also motivates charitable giving. New research reveals that focusing on karma increases generosity, but mostly toward individuals who suffer through no fault of their own.

articles.title: Honesty and openness are the primary personality traits that long-term couples share
articles.summary: Psychological research indicates that couples in lasting relationships do not necessarily have matching personalities. Instead, partners usually align on traits connected to their core values, such as honesty and openness, while differing in other areas.
articles.aiSummary: Psychological research indicates that couples in lasting relationships do not necessarily have matching personalities. Instead, partners usually align on traits connected to their core values, such as honesty and openness, while differing in other areas.

articles.title: How Can We Feel Loved If We Don’t Feel Known?
articles.summary: For Sonja, researching happiness means being asked over and over again, “What is the secret to happiness?” Sonja has always dreaded that question. It’s ridiculous, it’s reductive, and it’s restrictive. But mostly she has dreaded it because she has never had a solid, science-based answer that she could offer with any confidence. There is no secret to happiness. But there are huge contributors to happiness, and some of them are more powerful than others. Sonja resists being pinned down to just one. But if she were pressed to give an answer, she now has one. She found it when she met Harry, who s

articles.title: What It’s Like to Be…an Airline Pilot
articles.summary: Shooting cat-three landings in the fog, using the “voice of God” on unruly passengers, and declaring Mayday after an engine fire with Paul Drusch, a commercial airline pilot. Why does the pilot’s paycheck start with the parking brake? And what does “sterile cockpit” mean? (Spoiler: it doesn’t mean “clean.”) [buzzsprout episode='19294742' player='true'] View the episode transcript About What It's Like to Be... articles.title: What It’s Like to Be…a Clinical Ethicist
articles.summary: Untangling who gets to speak for a dying patient, weighing a treatment's benefits against its burdens, and searching for clarity in the grayest corners of healthcare with Esther Berkowitz, a clinical ethicist. What is the "dignity of risk"? And how do you know which "version" of a person to trust? [buzzsprout episode='19262736' player='true'] View the episode transcript About What It's Like to Be... articles.title: Announcing Our Summer Book Club and Podcast
articles.summary: Each summer, we read a novel and explore its themes through conversations with leading thinkers at the intersection of science and culture. This summer, we’ll read Flights by Polish author Olga Tokarczuk, the recipient of the 2018 Nobel Prize in literature. Flights is an enigma of a novel—part memoir, part short story collection, part ethnography of travel—that explores the human impulse toward movement; the perpetual tension between exploring somewhere new or staying put. “Clearly I did not inherit whatever gene it is that makes it so when you linger in a place you start to put down roots,” T

articles.title: Experts and the public have radically different visions of an automated future
articles.summary: A new survey finds that technology experts focus heavily on the benefits of artificial intelligence, while everyday users remain far more sensitive to its risks. This disconnect could lead to tools that fail to meet human needs.
articles.aiSummary: A new survey finds that technology experts focus heavily on the benefits of artificial intelligence, while everyday users remain far more sensitive to its risks. This disconnect could lead to tools that fail to meet human needs.

articles.title: Depression may be a stronger warning sign for dementia than insomnia
articles.summary: Older adults experiencing both depression and insomnia face an increased risk of developing dementia. However, a recent Journal of Affective Disorders study provides evidence that depressive symptoms are the most consistent indicator of future cognitive decline.
articles.aiSummary: Older adults experiencing both depression and insomnia face an increased risk of developing dementia. However, a recent Journal of Affective Disorders study provides evidence that depressive symptoms are the most consistent indicator of future cognitive decline.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Older adults experiencing both depression and insomnia face an increased risk of developing dementia. However, a recent Journal of Affective Disorders study provides evidence that depressive symptoms are the most consistent indicator of future cognitive decline.

articles.title: Intelligence found to predict prosocial behaviors like voting and charitable giving in massive study
articles.summary: A massive analysis of Swedish population records reveals that people with higher cognitive abilities are more likely to vote, donate to charity, and buy eco-friendly vehicles, suggesting general intelligence directly influences actions that benefit society.
articles.aiSummary: A massive analysis of Swedish population records reveals that people with higher cognitive abilities are more likely to vote, donate to charity, and buy eco-friendly vehicles, suggesting general intelligence directly influences actions that benefit society.

articles.title: Left-leaning Americans are driving the U.S. birth decline, new study finds
articles.summary: A new study reveals that left-leaning Americans are having significantly fewer children, driving the overall US fertility decline. Meanwhile, conservatives are maintaining steady birth rates, a demographic trend that could gradually shift the nation’s political landscape rightward.
articles.aiSummary: A new study reveals that left-leaning Americans are having significantly fewer children, driving the overall US fertility decline. Meanwhile, conservatives are maintaining steady birth rates, a demographic trend that could gradually shift the nation’s political landscape rightward.

articles.title: They knew the pill was fake but their memory still improved
articles.summary: Healthy older adults experienced measurable improvements in memory, physical performance, and stress after taking placebo pills for just three weeks. The most surprising finding was that the placebo often worked even when participants knew the pills were completely inactive.
articles.aiSummary: Healthy older adults experienced measurable improvements in memory, physical performance, and stress after taking placebo pills for just three weeks. The most surprising finding was that the placebo often worked even when participants knew the pills were completely inactive.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Healthy older adults experienced measurable improvements in memory, physical performance, and stress after taking placebo pills for just three weeks. The most surprising finding was that the placebo often worked even when participants knew the pills were completely inactive.

articles.title: Bilingual brains use a shared neural map to translate meaning across languages
articles.summary: New research reveals that bilingual brains rely on a shared geometric map to process meaning. While individual neurons treat languages differently, the overall neural structure remains the same, allowing seamless translation between languages.
articles.aiSummary: New research reveals that bilingual brains rely on a shared geometric map to process meaning. While individual neurons treat languages differently, the overall neural structure remains the same, allowing seamless translation between languages.
articles.aiSummaryEn: New research reveals that bilingual brains rely on a shared geometric map to process meaning. While individual neurons treat languages differently, the overall neural structure remains the same, allowing seamless translation between languages.

articles.title: The association between autistic traits and camouflaging is stronger in the general population
articles.summary: A comprehensive review found a strong link between autistic traits and the tendency to camouflage. Surprisingly, this connection appears stronger in the general population than among those formally diagnosed with autism.
articles.aiSummary: A comprehensive review found a strong link between autistic traits and the tendency to camouflage. Surprisingly, this connection appears stronger in the general population than among those formally diagnosed with autism.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A comprehensive review found a strong link between autistic traits and the tendency to camouflage. Surprisingly, this connection appears stronger in the general population than among those formally diagnosed with autism.

articles.title: Scientists may have finally found how Alzheimer's spreads through the brain
articles.summary: A common brain protein may be giving Alzheimer’s disease an unexpected way to spread, carrying toxic Tau proteins from damaged neurons into healthy ones. By blocking these harmful protein packages before they reach new cells, researchers believe it may one day be possible to slow the disease's relentless progression.
articles.aiSummary: A common brain protein may be giving Alzheimer’s disease an unexpected way to spread, carrying toxic Tau proteins from damaged neurons into healthy ones.
